subject: Physics
q: A capacitance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2 </mn><mi>μF</mi></math> is required in an electrical circuit across a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mtext>.</mtext><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kV</mi></math>. A large number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> capacitors are available which can withstand a potential difference of not more than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mn>0</mn><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The minimum number of capacitors required to achieve this is:
question: A capacitance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2 </mn><mi>μF</mi></math> is required in an electrical circuit across a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mtext>.</mtext><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kV</mi></math>. A large number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> capacitors are available which can withstand a potential difference of not more than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mn>0</mn><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The minimum number of capacitors required to achieve this is:
solution:
Let us assume that we connect n(whole number) capacitors in series such that the potential difference across the combination is 1000 V. This means the potential difference across each capacitor is

Vcapacitor=1000n V≤300 V

n⩾1000300=3.333.

nmin=4

This means we need to connect at least 4 capacitors in series to make sure that the potential across each one is less than 300 V.

Ceq=mnC=2 μF

m4=2⇒m=8

∴ Minimum no. of capacitors =8×4=32

subject: Physics
q: A capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>900</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is charged by a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery. The capacitor is disconnected from the battery and connected to another uncharged identical capacitor such that one plate of uncharged capacitor connected to positive plate and another plate of uncharged capacitor connected to negative plate of the charged capacitor. The loss of energy in this process is measured as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.  The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
question: A capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>900</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is charged by a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery. The capacitor is disconnected from the battery and connected to another uncharged identical capacitor such that one plate of uncharged capacitor connected to positive plate and another plate of uncharged capacitor connected to negative plate of the charged capacitor. The loss of energy in this process is measured as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.  The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
When a charged capacitor is connected to an uncharged capacitor, then there is loss of energy in the form of heat as charges flow from higher potential to lower potential.

So, heat ∆H=12C1C2C1+C2 V1-V22 

As the other capacitor is identical therefore charge is equally divided.

=12C22C(100-0)2

=12900×10-62×104=94 Joule

=2.25 Joule.

Hence, the value of x=225.

question: Two equal capacitors are first connected in series and then in parallel. The ratio of the equivalent capacities in the two cases will be:

For parallel combination,

subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance $12.5 \mathrm{pF}$ is charged by a battery connected between its plates to potential difference of $12.0 \mathrm{~V}$. The battery is now disconnected and a dielectric slab $\left(\epsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=6\right)$ is inserted between the plates. The change in its potential energy after inserting the dielectric slab is _____ $10^{-12} \mathrm{~J}$.
question: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance $12.5 \mathrm{pF}$ is charged by a battery connected between its plates to potential difference of $12.0 \mathrm{~V}$. The battery is now disconnected and a dielectric slab $\left(\epsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=6\right)$ is inserted between the plates. The change in its potential energy after inserting the dielectric slab is _____ $10^{-12} \mathrm{~J}$.
solution:
Before inserting dielectric capacitance is given $\mathrm{C}_0=12.5 \mathrm{pF}$ and charge on the capacitor $\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{C}_0 \mathrm{~V}$ After inserting dielectric capacitance will become $\epsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{C}_0$. Change in potential energy of the capacitor $\begin{aligned} & =\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{i}}-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{f}} \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2}{2 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}}-\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2}{2 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{f}}}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2}{2 \mathrm{C}_0}\left[1-\frac{1}{\epsilon_{\mathrm{r}}}\right] \\ & =\frac{\left(\mathrm{C}_0 \mathrm{~V}\right)^2}{2 \mathrm{C}_0}\left[1-\frac{1}{\epsilon_{\mathrm{r}}}\right]=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}_0 \mathrm{~V}^2\left[1-\frac{1}{\epsilon_{\mathrm{r}}}\right] \end{aligned}$
Using $\mathrm{C}_0=12.5 \mathrm{pF}, \mathrm{V}=12 \mathrm{~V}, \epsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=6$ $\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2}(12.5) \times 12^2\left[1-\frac{1}{6}\right]=\frac{1}{2}(12.5) \times 12^2 \times \frac{5}{6} \\ & =750 \mathrm{pJ}=750 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$

q: A capacitor $\mathrm{C}_1$ is charged up to a voltage $\mathrm{V}=$ $60 \mathrm{~V}$ by connecting it to battery $\mathrm{B}$ through switch (1), Now $\mathrm{C}_1$ is disconnected from battery and connected to a circuit consisting of two uncharged capacitors $\mathrm{C}_2=3.0 \mu \mathrm{F}$ and $\mathrm{C}_3=6.0 \mu \mathrm{F}$ through a switch $(2)$ as shown in the figure. The sum of final charges on $\mathrm{C}_2$ and $\mathrm{C}_3$ is:
solution: The sum of final charges on $\mathrm{C}_2$ and $\mathrm{C}_3$ is 36 $\mu \mathrm{C}$.

subject: Physics
q: Two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ are connected in parallel to a battery. Charge-time graph is shown below for the two capacitors. The energy stored with them are $U_1$ and $U_2$, respectively. Which of the given statements is true?
question: Two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ are connected in parallel to a battery. Charge-time graph is shown below for the two capacitors. The energy stored with them are $U_1$ and $U_2$, respectively. Which of the given statements is true?
solution:
For a capacitor at steady state
$q=C V \text { and } U=\frac{1}{2} C V^2$

Since $C_1$ and $C_2$ are connected in parallel, $V_1=V_2$.
Also from graph $q_1 < q_2$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad C_1 V_1 < C_2 V_2 \\
& \text { i.e. } C_1 < C_2 \text { or } C_2>C_1 \\
& \qquad \frac{U_1}{U_2}=\frac{C_1 V_1^2}{C_2 V_2^2}=\frac{C_1}{C_2} < 1
\end{aligned}$
or $U_1 < U_2$ or $U_2>U_1$

subject: Physics
q:
A parallel plate capacitor with plate area A and plate separation d is filled with a dielectric material of dielectric constant K=4. The thickness of the dielectric material is x, where x<d.

Let C1 and C2 be the capacitance of the system for x=13d and x=2d3, respectively. If C1=2 μF, the value of C2 is _____ μF.
question:
A parallel plate capacitor with plate area A and plate separation d is filled with a dielectric material of dielectric constant K=4. The thickness of the dielectric material is x, where x<d.

Let C1 and C2 be the capacitance of the system for x=13d and x=2d3, respectively. If C1=2 μF, the value of C2 is _____ μF.
solution:
The formula to calculate the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor having thickness d and with a dielectric of thickness x between the plates is given by

C=ε0Ad-x+xK   ...(1)

Substitute the values of the parameters for first case into equation (1) to obtain the capacitance.

C1=ε0A2d3+d34=4ε0A3d   ...(2)

Similarly, substitute the parameters into equation (1) for the second case to obtain the capacitance

C2=ε0Ad3+2d34=4ε0A2d=2ε0Ad   ...(3)

Divide equation (3) by equation (2) to obtain the capacitance in the second case.

C2C1=2ε0Ad4ε0A3d= 32C2=32C1   ...(4)

Substitute the value of the capacitance in the first case into equation (4) to obtain the value of the capacitance in the second case.

C2=32×2 μF= 3 μF

q: A force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> acts on a charged particle placed between two plates of a charged capacitor. If one plate of capacitor is removed, then the force acting on that particle will be.
question: A force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> acts on a charged particle placed between two plates of a charged capacitor. If one plate of capacitor is removed, then the force acting on that particle will be.
solution:
Electric field due to each plate, E1=E2=σ2ε0=Q2Aε0

Net electric field between the plates, Enet=E1+E2=QAε0

Force on charged particle between the plates, F1=qEnet=qQAε0=10 N

Now, in second case, the net electric field, E=σ2ε0 =Q2Aε0=5 N

Force on charged particle, F2=qE=qQ2Aε0=5 N

q: Three capacitors C1=2 μF, C2=6 μF and C3=12 μF are connected as shown in the figure. Find the ratio of the charges on capacitors C1, C2 and C3 respectively.
question: Three capacitors C1=2 μF, C2=6 μF and C3=12 μF are connected as shown in the figure. Find the ratio of the charges on capacitors C1, C2 and C3 respectively.
solution:
From the above circuit diagram, the equivalent capacitance of the combination of capacitors is, 

Ceq=C1+C2C3C2C3, 

Ceq=2+6×126+12=6 μF

Then, QC1QC2=C1VC2C3C2+C3V=2×(6+12)6×12=12 

and QC2=QC3

So, the ratio of charges are, 1:2:2.

question: Consider a parallel plate capacitor of area A (of each plate) and separation 'd' between the plates. If $E$ is the electric field and $\varepsilon_0$ is the permittivity of free space between the plates, then potential energy stored in the capacitor is

We know energy density
$\rho_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^2$
So potential energy $=\rho_{a v} \times$ Volume
$\Rightarrow \text { P.E. }=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^2 A d$

q: A series combination of $n_1$ capacitors, each of capacity $C_1$ is charged by source of potential difference $4 \mathrm{~V}$. When another parallel combination of $n_2$ capacitors each of capacity $C_2$ is charged by a source of potential difference $V$, it has the same total energy stored in it as the first combination has. The value of $C_2$ in terms of $C_1$ is then
question: A series combination of $n_1$ capacitors, each of capacity $C_1$ is charged by source of potential difference $4 \mathrm{~V}$. When another parallel combination of $n_2$ capacitors each of capacity $C_2$ is charged by a source of potential difference $V$, it has the same total energy stored in it as the first combination has. The value of $C_2$ in terms of $C_1$ is then
solution:
Equivalent capacitance of $n_2$ number of capacitors each of capacitance $\mathrm{C}_2$ in parallel $=n_2 C_2$
Equivalent capacitance of $n_1$ number of capacitors each of capacitances $\mathrm{C}_1$ in series.
Capacitance of each is $C_1=\frac{C_1}{n_1}$
According to question, total energy stored in both the combinations are same
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { i.e., } \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{C_1}{n_1}\right)(4 V)^2=\frac{1}{2}\left(n_2 C_2 \text { b }^2\right. \\
& \therefore C_2=\frac{16 C_1}{n_1 n_2} \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

q: A parallel-plate capacitor of capacitance $40 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ is connected to a 100 V power supply. Now the intermediate space between the plates is filled with a dielectric material of dielectric constant $\mathrm{K}=2$. Due to the introduction of dielectric material, the extra charge and the change in the electrostatic energy in the capacitor, respectively, are
question: A parallel-plate capacitor of capacitance $40 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ is connected to a 100 V power supply. Now the intermediate space between the plates is filled with a dielectric material of dielectric constant $\mathrm{K}=2$. Due to the introduction of dielectric material, the extra charge and the change in the electrostatic energy in the capacitor, respectively, are
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Delta \mathrm{q}=(\mathrm{KC}-\mathrm{C}) \mathrm{V} \\ & =40 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \\ & =4000 \times 10^{-3}=4 \mathrm{mC} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}^{\prime} \mathrm{V}^2-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}^2=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{~K}-1) \mathrm{CV}^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}^2(2-1) \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}^2=\frac{1}{2} \times 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 10000 \\ & =0.2 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

12CV2=12LImax2   ...1

Imax=CLV=100×10-66.4×10-3×12=128=1.5 A

subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate air capacitor is connected to a battery. The plates are pulled apart at uniform speed $v$. If $x$ is the separation between the plates at any instant, then the time rate of change of electrostatic energy of the capacitor is proportional to $x^\alpha$, where $\alpha$ is _____.
question: A parallel plate air capacitor is connected to a battery. The plates are pulled apart at uniform speed $v$. If $x$ is the separation between the plates at any instant, then the time rate of change of electrostatic energy of the capacitor is proportional to $x^\alpha$, where $\alpha$ is _____.
solution:
The electrostatic energy of a parallel plate capacitor is given by $U = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$.

For a parallel plate capacitor, the capacitance is $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{x}$, where $x$ is the separation between the plates.

Since the capacitor remains connected to the battery, the potential difference $V$ across the plates is constant.

Substituting $C$ into the energy equation gives:

$U = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2x}$

Differentiating $U$ with respect to time $t$ to find the time rate of change of electrostatic energy:

$\dfrac{dU}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt} \left( \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2x} \right) = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2x^2} \dfrac{dx}{dt}$

Given that the plates are pulled apart at a uniform speed $v$, we have $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = v$.

$\dfrac{dU}{dt} = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2 v}{2x^2}$

From the above expression, it is clear that $\dfrac{dU}{dt} \propto x^{-2}$.

Comparing this with $x^\alpha$, we get $\alpha = -2$.

Answer: $-2$

One of them is charged to the potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> and other to the potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>V</mi></math>. subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate capacitor has capacitance $C$, when there is vacuum within the parallel plates. A sheet having thickness $\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{\mathrm{rd}}$ of the separation between the plates and relative permittivity $K$ is introduced between the plates. The new capacitance of the system is :
question: A parallel plate capacitor has capacitance $C$, when there is vacuum within the parallel plates. A sheet having thickness $\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{\mathrm{rd}}$ of the separation between the plates and relative permittivity $K$ is introduced between the plates. The new capacitance of the system is :
solution:
Original capacitance: $C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$
After inserting dielectric of thickness $\frac{d}{3}$, system becomes two capacitors in series.
Vacuum region: $C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{2d/3} = \frac{3C}{2}$
Dielectric region: $C_2 = \frac{K\varepsilon_0 A}{d/3} = 3KC$
Equivalent capacitance: $\frac{1}{C_{new}} = \frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_2} = \frac{2}{3C} + \frac{1}{3KC} = \frac{2K+1}{3KC}$
$C_{new} = \frac{3KC}{2K+1}$

q: In the given figure the total charge stored in the combination of capacitors is 100 µC. The value of ‘x’ is ______________.
question: In the given figure the total charge stored in the combination of capacitors is 100 µC. The value of ‘x’ is ______________.
solution:
All the capacitors are in parallel combination and therefore potential drop across each capacitor would be the same.

Charge on the capacitor can be written as, Q=CV.

The charge on C1 is Q1=2×10=20 μC

The charge on C2 is Q2=x×10=10x μC

The charge on C3 is Q3=3×10=30 μC

By the given data.

C1+C2+C3=100 μC

⇒102+x+3=100

⇒x=5

q: A parallel plate capacitor having capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>pF</mi></math> is charged by a battery to a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> between its plates. The charging battery is now disconnected and a porcelain slab of dielectric constant <math><mn>6.5</mn></math> is slipped between the plates. The work done by the capacitor on the slab is
question: A parallel plate capacitor having capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>pF</mi></math> is charged by a battery to a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> between its plates. The charging battery is now disconnected and a porcelain slab of dielectric constant <math><mn>6.5</mn></math> is slipped between the plates. The work done by the capacitor on the slab is
solution:
Initial energy of capacitor

Ui=12q2c
=12×120×12012=600 pJ

Since battery is disconnected so charge remain same. Final energy of capacitor

 Uf=12q2c k

=12×120×12012×6.5=92 pJ

W+Uf=Ui 
W=508 pJ

subject: Physics
q: A capacitor is discharging through a resistor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. Consider in time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, the energy stored in the capacitor reduces to half of its initial value and in time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, the charge stored reduces to one eighth of its initial value. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> will be
question: A capacitor is discharging through a resistor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. Consider in time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, the energy stored in the capacitor reduces to half of its initial value and in time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, the charge stored reduces to one eighth of its initial value. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> will be
solution:
We know that in the discharging circuit the charge is given by,

q=q0e-tRC  ⇒tRC=lnq0q

In time t1 the energy stored reduces to half. Hence, q122C=12×q022C⇒q0q1=2. Therefore,

t1RC=ln2=12ln2.

In time t2 the charge stored reduces to 18th of initial value. Hence, q2q0=18. Therefore,

t2RC=ln8=3ln2.

Hence,

t1t2=12ln23ln2=16

q: A parallel plate capacitor of area $60 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ and separation $3 \mathrm{~mm}$ is charged initially to $90 \mu \mathrm{C}$. If the medium between the plate gets slightly conducting and the plate loses the charge initially at the rate of $2.5 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{s}$, then what is the magnetic field between the plates ?
question: A parallel plate capacitor of area $60 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ and separation $3 \mathrm{~mm}$ is charged initially to $90 \mu \mathrm{C}$. If the medium between the plate gets slightly conducting and the plate loses the charge initially at the rate of $2.5 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{s}$, then what is the magnetic field between the plates ?
solution: Magnetic field between the plates in this case is zero.

subject: Physics
q: Two identical thin metal plates has charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The plates were brought close to each other to form a parallel plate capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math>. The potential difference between them is :
question: Two identical thin metal plates has charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The plates were brought close to each other to form a parallel plate capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math>. The potential difference between them is :
solution:
On bringing the charged metal plates closer, electric field E→ in the intervening space is E→=E1→+E2→

Where

Intensity of field due plate charged by q1 is E1→ =σ12ε0 = q12ε0A(directed rightwards)

And Intensity of field due to plate charged by q2 is 

E2→ =σ22ε0 = q22ε0A (directed leftwards)
So, Net field is given by 

E→=E1→+E2→ ⇒ E = E1- E2

∴ E =q12ε0A-q22ε0A=q1-q22ε0A   ...1

For parallel plate capacitor C = ε0Ad   ...2

From above two equations 

E=q1-q22Cd   ...3 

∵ Relation between intensity of field E and potential difference V between the plates is 

E = Vd   ...4

From equation 3 and 4

V = q1-q22C

q:
The electric field in an electromagnetic wave is given as
E→=20sinωt-xcj→N C-1, where ω and c are angular frequency and velocity of electromagnetic wave respectively. The energy contained in a volume of 5×10-4 m3 will be
(Given ε0=8.85×10-12C2 N-1 m-2)
question:
The electric field in an electromagnetic wave is given as
E→=20sinωt-xcj→N C-1, where ω and c are angular frequency and velocity of electromagnetic wave respectively. The energy contained in a volume of 5×10-4 m3 will be
(Given ε0=8.85×10-12C2 N-1 m-2)
solution:
Given that, E→=20sinωt-xcj→N C-1

The average energy density of an electromagnetic wave is 12ε0E20.

The total energy stored is 

Etotal =12ε0E02× volume

⇒Etotal=12×8.85×10-12×(20)2×5×10-4=8.85×10-13 J

question: The charge stored by the capacitor $C$ in the given circuit in the steady state is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mu \mathrm{C}$.
solution:
In steady state:

$i = 2.5/5 = 0.5$ A
$V_c = 4 \times 0.5$
$V_c = 2V$
charge
$Q = CV_c$
$= 5 \times 2$
$= 10 \, \mu C$

subject: Physics
q: A capacitor $P$ with capacitance $10 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}$ is fully charged with a potential difference of 6.0 V and disconnected from the battery. The charged capacitor $P$ is connected across another capacitor $Q$ with capacitance $20 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}$. The charge on capacitor $Q$ when equilibrium is established will be $\alpha \times 10^{-5} C$ (assume capacitor $Q$ does not have any charge initially), the value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$。
question: A capacitor $P$ with capacitance $10 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}$ is fully charged with a potential difference of 6.0 V and disconnected from the battery. The charged capacitor $P$ is connected across another capacitor $Q$ with capacitance $20 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}$. The charge on capacitor $Q$ when equilibrium is established will be $\alpha \times 10^{-5} C$ (assume capacitor $Q$ does not have any charge initially), the value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$。
solution:
Capacitor P (10 × 10⁻⁶ F) is charged to 6.0 V.
Initial charge: $Q_P = 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 6 = 60 \times 10^{-6}$ C.
When connected to uncharged capacitor Q (20 × 10⁻⁶ F), charge redistributes until both reach same potential.
Final voltage: $V_f = \frac{Q_{total}}{C_{total}} = \frac{60 \times 10^{-6}}{30 \times 10^{-6}} = 2$ V.
Charge on Q: $Q_Q = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 = 40 \times 10^{-6}$ C = $4 \times 10^{-5}$ C.
Thus $\alpha = 4$.

subject: Physics
q:
Two parallel plate capacitors C1 and C2 each having capacitance of 10 μF are individually charged by a 100 V D.C. source. Capacitor C1 is kept connected to the source and a dielectric slab is inserted between it plates. Capacitor C2 is disconnected from the source and then a dielectric slab is inserted in it. Afterwards the capacitor C1 is also disconnected from the source and the two capacitors are finally connected in parallel combination. The common potential of the combination will be _____ V.

(Assuming Dielectric constant =10)
question:
Two parallel plate capacitors C1 and C2 each having capacitance of 10 μF are individually charged by a 100 V D.C. source. Capacitor C1 is kept connected to the source and a dielectric slab is inserted between it plates. Capacitor C2 is disconnected from the source and then a dielectric slab is inserted in it. Afterwards the capacitor C1 is also disconnected from the source and the two capacitors are finally connected in parallel combination. The common potential of the combination will be _____ V.

(Assuming Dielectric constant =10)
solution:
As source is connected in the process therefore, additional charge will be provided. Therefore, charge on C1=KCV.

Since source is disconnected, so its charge will remain the same. Then, charge on C2=CV.

New capacitance in both cases will become, KC.

When they are connected in parallel charge will be equally divided so charge on one capacitor is q=K+12CV.

So common potential,

V'=qKC=K+12KV=55 V.

question: In the figure is shown a system of four capacitors connected across a 10 V battery. The charge that will flow from switch S when it is closed is:
solution:

q1=2×5=10 μC

q2=3×5=15 μC

=15-10=5 μC

Charge 5 μC flows from b to a
